posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
This production chart is pretty dramatic.

I’d love to have the underlying data and chart, say, a five-year trailing average. It looks like it would be a downward line for the past 40 years, though some of that, no doubt, is a reduction in plonk production as France faced more competition from Spain, Portugal and Italy.

twitter.com

AAWE

@wineecon
French 2021 wine harvest estimated to be lowest in decades; even lower than 1991 and 2017. Mainly due to the spring frost damage, Agreste, the statistical service of France’s Ag ministry, expects a harvest of 33.3m hectoliters, i.e., 29% less than 2020. agreste.agriculture.gouv.fr/agreste-web/do… https://t.co/h7kb78XDF8
4:47 PM - 8 Sep 2021 34 15

posts.name: Henry Brown
posts.cooked: could be wrong but I think 21 is also not a good vintage in France, given the summer it feels like we’ve had. 2017 prices did drop a bit but there is a LOT of stock out there still - if they want to actuaally sell through the wines EP (which is not necessarily the goal), they’ll haave to lower prices.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
I’m wondering whether similar charts from most other countries would run parallel or inversely proportional. This is Portugal’s last 12 years (sans Madeira and Azores) in hl:

portugal.jpg

I can’t decipher those numbers in that form.

As I recall, there were EU incentives to pull up some vines, no? Obviously, no one on the Cote d’Or is doing that, but perhaps in some more marginal areas.

posts.name: Tomás Costa
posts.cooked:
I’m wondering whether similar charts from most other countries would run parallel or inversely proportional. This is Portugal’s last 12 years (sans Madeira and Azores) in hl:

portugal.jpg

I can’t decipher those numbers in that form.

As I recall, there were EU incentives to pull up some vines, no? Obviously, no one on the Cote d’Or is doing that, but perhaps in some more marginal areas.

My bad. The number furthest to the right is 2009, the one furthest to the left is 2021. The 99 next to all numbers stands for 99%, which is the percentage of wine that production that comes from continental Portugal (the original chart includes a breakdown by region and their corresponding percentages).

EU incentives improved wine production over here, quality-wise, but discouraged other types of agricultural production. Not sure what happened in France, as the funds usually went to the poorest regions of newly entered countries.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
could be wrong but I think 21 is also not a good vintage in France, given the summer it feels like we’ve had. 2017 prices did drop a bit but there is a LOT of stock out there still - if they want to actuaally sell through the wines EP (which is not necessarily the goal), they’ll haave to lower prices.

It’s a little premature to say that, before the grapes have even been harvested in most regions.

In Burgundy, it’s certain to be heterogenous after the Spring frosts, but for the best farmers there is still everything to play for to produce great wines.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:

portugal.jpg

I can’t decipher those numbers in that form.

As I recall, there were EU incentives to pull up some vines, no? Obviously, no one on the Cote d’Or is doing that, but perhaps in some more marginal areas.

My bad. The number furthest to the right is 2009, the one furthest to the left is 2021. The 99 next to all numbers stands for 99%, which is the percentage of wine that production that comes from continental Portugal (the original chart includes a breakdown by region and their corresponding percentages).

EU incentives improved wine production over here, quality-wise, but discouraged other types of agricultural production. Not sure what happened in France, as the funds usually went to the poorest regions of newly entered countries.

As I recall, French production began to fall after ES and PT joined the EU. Their entry was bad for growers and winemakers in the Midi. But that’s a dim recollection.

posts.name: Henry Brown
posts.cooked:
could be wrong but I think 21 is also not a good vintage in France, given the summer it feels like we’ve had. 2017 prices did drop a bit but there is a LOT of stock out there still - if they want to actuaally sell through the wines EP (which is not necessarily the goal), they’ll haave to lower prices.

It’s a little premature to say that, before the grapes have even been harvested in most regions.

In Burgundy, it’s certain to be heterogenous after the Spring frosts, but for the best farmers there is still everything to play for to produce great wines.

I’ll take your steer on it as you’re undoubtedly more of an expert than me, but I’m looking at past vintages in memory that I recall similar weather - e.g. 2013 and 2017. Every time I check the weather in Bordeaux it looks poor, rainy, and lacking a sustained hot and sunny spell.

Either way, though, it might be a vintaage that produces some great wines but I dont see the pressure on it to sell out if it comes out at an even comparable price to 18/19/20, which comes back to my original point - I think a lowering of prices is sensible.

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked:
It’s hot and damp in Bordeaux.

It’s not looking promising.

posts.name: Jeff M 0 L l
posts.cooked: Will look to see what’s still out there for 2020 EP in another month or two. May add a few bottles if 21 is the end of this nice run.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
Having been in the Bordeaux area, I agree with Russell that it’s not looking good, what with frost and mildew. We have been having a short heatwave over the last few days, the first really hot temperatures since early July. Where we live near Paris, it has been the coldest and wettest summer I can remember. My barometer is my own grapes - there are very few and the vines look terrible. Likewise, the entire tomato crop rotted. However, William is right that it’s never over until the fat lady sings.

Over here, some Bordeaux 2019s are starting to hit the shelves and if I was to stock up, these would be my chosen targets. The shop prices are so far about 10% lower than EP, which was already a lot lower than 2018, so there are some good deals to be made.

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked:
And it’s wet today and for most of the next week.

I have stopped asking people how things are going.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Either way, though, it might be a vintaage that produces some great wines but I dont see the pressure on it to sell out if it comes out at an even comparable price to 18/19/20, which comes back to my original point - I think a lowering of prices is sensible.

If quantities were normal, that might make sense. But if crops are down by half or more, it’s hard to see how prices will come down.

posts.name: Henry Brown
posts.cooked:
Either way, though, it might be a vintaage that produces some great wines but I dont see the pressure on it to sell out if it comes out at an even comparable price to 18/19/20, which comes back to my original point - I think a lowering of prices is sensible.

If quantities were normal, that might make sense. But if crops are down by half or more, it’s hard to see how prices will come down.

Depends really if they want to sit on stock or sell it, really. For a normal system/retailer, they’d price to sell - so prices will have to come down simply on the basis of not having the quality (assuming that is true).

I’m not going to do an exhaustive check, but for example 2016 Palmer en primeur was £1440/6 (ouch) from the one email I have, 2017 Palmer was £1175/6.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
Palmer is one of those estates that adjusts prices according to vintage or market conditions, although after what happened with 2019, they may look to the long term, and keep a steadier pricing.

But I don’t see a short 2021 vintage of mediocrity helping my chances in the bet.

posts.name: Henry Brown
posts.cooked:
Palmer was pulled at random - I bet I could replicate that fact across many chateau.

2017 Lafite was £2435/6, Haut Brion £2025/6, LMHB £1380/6, Ducru £744/6

2016 Lafite £2900/6, Haut Brion £2485/6, LMHB £1995/6, £852/6

as examples

The only counter I’ve found so far is Canon - but obviously they’re on an aggressive price curve since 2014 anyway.
